1: uzu benei Binyamin miqerev Yerushalaim (ʿuzzû bĕnê-binyāmîn miqqereb yĕrûšālaim): 'Flee to safety, people of Benjamin — get out of Jerusalem!' Jeremiah himself was from Benjamin (1:1 — Anathoth is in Benjamin). The address to Benjamin adds personal weight: these are Jeremiah's own tribe. Tekoa was the home of Amos (Amos 1:1); its mention may evoke prophetic urgency. Beth-haccerem (perhaps modern Ramat Rachel) is near Jerusalem — the smoke signal (mas'et esh) over it announces the enemy's location. Ra'ah nishqefah mitzafon (disaster threatens from the north) repeats the refrain of 4:6 and 1:14-15.
2: hanavah vehaduqah dimmiti bat-tzion (hannāwāh wĕhaddaqqāh dimmîtî bat-ṣiyyôn): 'I will destroy the fair and delicate Daughter Zion.' Navah (pleasant, beautiful — used of Jerusalem's beauty in Ps 48:2; Lam 1:6) and duqah (delicate/pampered — suggesting luxury and ease) describe Jerusalem at the height of her perceived security. The image of the beautiful pampered woman who will be destroyed inverts the 'Daughter Zion' motif used both for comfort (Isa 52:2) and for lament (Lam 1:6; 2:1-4). Shepherds and their flocks camping around her (v. 3) is the siege imagery: the besieging army encampments with their 'tents' and 'pasturing' divisions.
3: ro'im uviqram yavo'u eilah taqe'u aleha ohalim (rōʿîm ûḇiqrām yābōʾû ʾēlêhā tāqĕʿû ʿālêhā ʾōhālîm): 'Shepherds with their flocks will come to her and pitch their tents around her.' The enemy commanders are metaphorically 'shepherds' controlling their flocks (troops). The 'pasturing each his portion' (ra'u ish yado) describes the division of the city among the besieging contingents for systematic plunder. The ironic use of pastoral language for military siege — peaceful shepherd images for violent conquest — intensifies the horror.
4: qaddeshu aleiha milhamah qumu vena'aleh batzohorayim (qaddĕšû ʿālêhā milḥāmāh qûmû wĕnaʿăleh baṣṣŏhŏrayim): 'Prepare for holy war against her; arise, let us attack at noon.' Qaddeshu milhamah (prepare/consecrate holy war) uses the same vocabulary as Israel's own holy-war tradition (Josh 3:5; Joel 4:9). The enemy consecrates their war against Jerusalem using Israel's own ritual vocabulary — a dark irony. The lament 'Alas, the day is fading' (oy lanu ki fanah hayom) comes from the enemy's mouth: they began their assault at noon but have not yet taken the city; night is coming. They resolve to continue through the night (v. 5).
5: qumu vena'aleh ballaylah vena'ashet armenoteiha (qûmû wĕnaʿăleh ballaylāh wĕnaśḥîtāh ʾarmĕnôtêhā): 'Let us rise up and attack by night and demolish her fortified towers.' Armonot (fortified towers/palaces) are the elite residential structures and citadel towers — the symbols of Jerusalem's power and pride. Their demolition is the visible sign of conquest. Night attacks in ancient warfare were uncommon and desperate — the enemy's willingness to fight through the night signals their determination.
6: ki-koh amar YHWH Tzvaot kirtzu etzah vishfecho al-Yerushalaim solelah (kî-kōh ʾāmar Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt kirṭĕzû ʿēṣāh wĕšipĕkû ʿal-yĕrûšālaim sōlĕlāh): 'Cut down trees and build a siege ramp against Jerusalem.' The siege ramp (solelah) is an earthwork built up against the city wall to enable assault. The same technique Babylon used historically against Jerusalem and Tyre (Ezek 26:8). The diagnosis: 'within her is nothing but oppression' (hi ha'ir haquqad kullah ashaq beqirbah) — the oshaq (oppression/exploitation) within is the cause that brings the siege without.
7: kehakin bor meimav ken hiqruh rah (kĕhaqîr bôr mêmêhā kēn hiqĕrāh rāʿāh): 'As a well keeps pouring out water, so she keeps pouring out wickedness.' The artesian-well image for persistent sin: the well produces water without stopping or requiring effort; Jerusalem produces wickedness with the same effortless constancy. Chamas ve-shod (violence and devastation) — the same word-pair used of the reason for Noah's flood (Gen 6:11, 13: chamas). Before YHWH continually (negdi tamid): the sin is performed as if YHWH is not watching, but it is constantly before his eyes.
8: hivvaseeri Yerushalaim pen-tiqah nafshi mimmech (hiwwāsĕrî yĕrûšālaim pen-tiqaʿ nafšî mimmēk): 'Take warning, O Jerusalem, or I will turn away from you.' Hiwwaseri (be warned/corrected — take the discipline) is the verb from musar (correction/discipline) — the same root as the wisdom tradition's instruction. The threat is not destruction but abandonment: pen-tiqah nafshi mimmech (lest I turn away from you). The departure of YHWH's presence (nephesh, divine soul/self) is worse than judgment; it is the theological catastrophe that Ezekiel will actually portray happening in chs. 8-11.
9: kaleh yiglellu sha'erit Yisrael ke-gannan al-hagafen (kālēh yeʾolĕlû šĕʾērît yiśrāʾēl kĕbōṣēr ʿal-haggāpen): 'Carefully glean the remnant of Israel as a vinedresser gleans a vine.' The gleaning (olel) metaphor: after the main harvest, harvesters passed through the vineyard again picking the overlooked clusters. By Deuteronomic law (Deut 24:21; Lev 19:10) the gleanings were left for the poor. Here YHWH commands a thorough gleaning — even the 'remnant of Israel' (she'erit Yisrael) will be gathered up in the judgment, leaving nothing behind on the vine. The remnant language is double-edged: elsewhere (Jer 23:3; Isa 10:20-21) the remnant is the saved group, but here it is the group targeted for thorough judgment.
10: al-mi adabera ve'a'idah veyishme'u hine arlatah oznam (ʾel-mî ʾădabbĕrāh wĕʾāʿîdāh wĕyišmāʿû hinnēh ʿorlātāh ʾoznām): 'To whom shall I speak and give warning so that they will listen? Look, their ears are uncircumcised.' Arlatah oznam (uncircumcised ears) is the auditory parallel to 4:4's uncircumcised heart. The physical sign of the Abrahamic covenant (circumcision) becomes an image for spiritual receptivity in multiple registers: ear (6:10), heart (4:4; Deut 10:16), and lips (Exod 6:12, 30 — 'uncircumcised lips' = poor speaker). The word of YHWH has become 'an object of scorn' (cherepah) to them — they take no pleasure in it.
11: veth chemath YHWH male'ti nile'iti hakil (wĕʾēt ḥămat Yhwh māleʾtî nilʾêtî hākêl): 'So I am full of the LORD's fury; I am worn out with holding it in.' The image of Jeremiah as a vessel filled with divine wrath too full to be contained reverses Isa 6:8 where the prophet volunteers; here Jeremiah is compelled by the weight of what he carries. Nile'iti hakil (worn out with holding it in / exhausted from restraint) — the prophet cannot remain silent because the divine message has overpowered his resistance (cf. 20:9: 'if I say I will not mention him ... his word is in my heart like a burning fire shut up in my bones').
12: venasabbu bateihem le'acherim sadot unnashim yachdav (wĕnāsabbû bāttêhem lĕʾăḥērîm śādôt ûnāšîm yaḥdāw): 'Their houses will be given to others, along with their fields and wives.' The transfer of houses, fields, and wives is the covenant curse explicitly listed in Deut 28:30: 'you will build a house but not live in it ... you will plant a vineyard but not use it ... your wife will be given to another.' Jeremiah is announcing the historical actualization of the Deuteronomic covenant curses.
13: ki miqatanam ad-gedolam kulloh botze' vatza vemin-navi ve'ad-kohen kulloh oseh sheqer (kî miqqĕṭannām wĕʿad-gĕdôlām kullô bōṣēaʿ bāṣaʿ ûminnābîʾ wĕʿad-kōhēn kullô ʿōśeh šāqer): 'For from the least to the greatest of them, everyone is greedy for dishonest gain; from prophet to priest, everyone acts deceitfully.' Botze' vatza (cutting off a cut / greedy for gain) — the doubled form intensifies the avarice: covetous through and through. The phrase 'from the least to the greatest' (min-qatanam ad-gedolam) makes the corruption universal across social strata, and 'from prophet to priest' makes it universal across religious authorities. This verse is quoted in 8:10b (nearly verbatim) — it frames both chs. 6 and 8 with the same diagnosis.
14: vayirpe'u et-shever bat-ami al-neqalah lemor shalom shalom veyn shalom (wayrappĕʾû ʾet-šeber bat-ʿammî ʿal-nĕqallāh lēmōr šālôm šālôm wĕʾên šālôm): 'They treat the wound of my people as though it were minor, saying, 'Peace, peace,' when there is no peace.' The doubled shalom shalom is the grammatical intensifier — 'complete peace, total peace' — that makes the false assurance more emphatic, not less. The verb repha (heal/treat) applied to shever (breaking/fracture) means treating a serious break superficially: the false prophets apply a bandage to a fracture that requires surgery. This verse is the most quoted Jeremiah phrase in NT secondary literature on false prophecy (cf. Ezek 13:10: 'they have misled my people, saying Peace, when there is no peace'). Paul's 'peace and safety' formula in 1 Thess 5:3 draws on this exact prophetic tradition.
15: hahivoshu ki to'evah asu gam bosh lo yevoshu gam hachliim lo yad'u (hăhôbîšû kî tôʿēbāh ʿāśû gam-bōš lōʾ-yēbōšû gam-haklîm lōʾ yādāʿû): 'Are they ashamed of their detestable conduct? No, they have no shame at all; they do not even know how to blush.' The conscience has been anesthetized: bosh (be ashamed) and hachlim (blush) are the two natural responses of moral awareness to violation. Their absence marks the progressive hardening that Jeremiah diagnoses (3:3: 'you have the forehead of a prostitute; you refuse to be ashamed'). The parallelism with Zeph 3:5 ('the unjust knows no shame') and with Paul's 'their conscience is seared' (1 Tim 4:2) identifies a recurring diagnostic of moral deterioration.
16: koh amar YHWH imdu al-drakhim ure'u ushalu lenatevot olam ei-ze derekh hatov velkhu vah umtze'u margoa lenapshekhem (kōh ʾāmar Yhwh ʿimĕdû ʿal-dĕrākîm ûrĕʾû wĕšaʾălû lĕnĕtîbôt ʿôlām ʾê-zeh derek haṭṭôb wĕlĕkû-bāh ûmĕṣĕʾû margeʿā lĕnapšĕkem): 'Stand at the crossroads and look; ask for the ancient paths, ask where the good way is, and walk in it. You will find rest for your souls.' Netivot olam (paths of the age / ancient paths) are the time-tested, traditional ways of covenant faithfulness — not arbitrary conservatism but the accumulated wisdom of what has proven to lead to life. The phrase margoa lenaphshechem (rest for your souls) is quoted almost verbatim by Jesus in Matt 11:29: 'you will find rest for your souls.' Jesus does not cite a path but presents himself as the path — 'I am the way' (John 14:6) — replacing the question 'where is the good way?' with the answer 'I am the way.' Israel's response here — 'we will not walk in it' — is exactly the response Jesus anticipates from his own generation.
17: vehaqimoti aleichem tzofim (wăhaqqimōtî ʿălêkem ṣōfîm): 'I appointed watchmen over you and said, 'Listen to the sound of the trumpet!'' Tzofim (watchmen) is the prophetic self-designation par excellence (Ezek 3:17; 33:7: 'I have made you a watchman for the house of Israel'). The watchman's role: sound the alarm when danger approaches. The trumpet sound is the same covenant alarm of v. 1 and 4:5. Israel's response — 'we will not listen' — creates the culpability: they were warned through YHWH's appointed watchmen and explicitly refused. Ezekiel 33:4-5 develops the watchman logic: if the watchman warns and the person refuses to heed, their blood is on their own head.
18: lachen shime'u hagoyim ude'i ha'edah et-asher bam (lākēn šimĕʿû haggôyim ûdĕʿî hāʿēdāh ʾēt ʾăšer-bām): 'Therefore, you nations, hear; you witnesses, know what has happened among them.' The nations are summoned as a courtroom audience — the covenant lawsuit (rib) of YHWH against Israel goes public before the international community. This is the humiliation Jeremiah warned about in 4:16: the nations hear of Jerusalem's shame. The nations-as-witnesses motif appears throughout the prophets (Mic 1:2; Isa 34:1) where cosmic observation of covenant judgment establishes YHWH's justice before all peoples.
19: shime'i ha'aretz hineni mevi ra'ah el-ha'am hazeh peri machshevotam (šimĕʿî hāʾāreṣ hinnĕnî mēbîʾ rāʿāh ʾel-hāʿam hazzeh pĕrî maḥšĕbôtām): 'Listen, O earth: I am going to bring disaster on this people — the direct result of their own schemes.' Peri machshevotam (the fruit/result of their schemes): the disaster is organically connected to the sin, as fruit to its tree. The word peri (fruit) establishes the agricultural metaphor of consequential connection — what you plant bears its own kind of fruit. The rejection of Torah (torati, my law) and the words (devarai) of YHWH is the specific cause named.
20: lamah-zeh li levonah misheva tavo uqaneh hatov meeretz merhaq (lāmmāh-zeh lî lĕbōnāh miššĕbāʾ tābōʾ ûqāneh haṭṭôb mēʾereṣ mêrḥāq): 'What good is it if incense is brought to me from Sheba and fragrant cane from a distant land?' Sheba (Arabia/Ethiopia) was famous for spices and incense. The two items — levonah (frankincense, the purest liturgical incense) and qaneh hatov (sweet calamus/fragrant cane) — represent the most expensive and exotic offerings available. Their rejection underlines that YHWH's complaint is not about the quality of the offerings but their moral context: 'your burnt offerings are not acceptable ... your sacrifices do not please me.' The same critique as Isa 1:11-17 and Amos 5:21-24: ritual without justice is abomination.
21: lachen koh-amar YHWH hinneni noten el-ha'am hazeh mikhsholim (lākēn kōh-ʾāmar Yhwh hinnĕnî nōtēn ʾel-hāʿam hazzeh mikšōlîm): 'Therefore this is what the LORD says: I am going to put obstacles before this people, and they will stumble over them.' Mikhsholim (stumbling blocks/obstacles) is used both literally and figuratively for whatever causes covenant Israel to fall (Ezek 7:19; 14:3-4). That fathers and sons, neighbors and friends 'all perish together' (yachadu yovadu) captures the social solidarity of covenant judgment: when a society's leaders and people together have rejected YHWH, the consequences are shared across all relationships.
22: hinei am ba meeretz tzafon vegoi gadol yeur miyarketei aretz (hinnēh ʿam bāʾ mēʾereṣ ṣāpôn wĕgôy gādôl yēʿôr miyyarkĕtê ʾāreṣ): 'Look, an army is coming from the northern country, a great nation is being roused from the far ends of the earth.' The northern foe (eretz tzafon) of 1:13-15 and 4:6 is here a 'great nation' (goi gadol) stirred from the furthest reaches of the earth. Yeur (roused/stirred up) — as one arouses a sleeping person or stirs up a fire. YHWH is the one rousing the enemy (cf. Isa 45:13: 'I will rouse him up in righteousness'); the enemy is YHWH's instrument, not an autonomous threat.
23: qeshet vekidon yachaziqo akhzari hu velo yerachemu (qešet wĕkîdôn yaḥăzîqû ʾakzārî hûʾ wĕlōʾ yĕraḥēmû): 'They are armed with bow and spear; they are ruthless and without mercy.' The three-fold characterization — akhzari (ruthless/cruel), lo yerachemu (without mercy), and the sea-roaring sound of their approach — strips away any human quality from the invaders. The comparison to the sea ('they sound like the roaring sea') echoes the chaos-sea imagery of the psalms and Isaiah where the nations are compared to the raging sea (Ps 65:7; Isa 17:12-13). The address 'O Daughter Zion' shifts the personification to the recipient of this unstoppable force.
24: shama'nu et-shim'o rafu yadeynu tzarah heheziqatnu chil keyoledah (šāmaʿnû ʾet-šimʿô rāpû yādênû ṣārāh hĕḥezîqātnû ḥîl kĕyôlēdāh): 'We have heard the report — our hands go limp; distress has seized us, pain like a woman in labor.' The first-person plural shifts the perspective to the Judean civilian response. 'Hands going limp' (rafu yadeynu) is the paralysis of terror, the same idiom as Rahab's report about Canaan's response to Israel: 'our hearts melted and everyone's courage failed' (Josh 2:11). The birth-pain metaphor (chil keyoledah) for the pain of invasion creates the eschatological resonance: the same image in 1 Thess 5:3 ('sudden destruction comes upon them like labor pains') draws on this Jeremianic tradition.
25: al-tetze'u hasadeh vevadarech al-tele'chu ki cherev le-oyev magor misaviv (ʾal-tēṣĕʾû haśśādeh wĕbadderek ʾal-tēlēkû kî ḥereb lĕʾôyēb māgôr missābîb): 'Do not go out into the fields or walk along the roads, for the enemy is armed with a sword; terror is on every side.' Magor misaviv (terror/fear on every side) becomes Jeremiah's own signature phrase, thrown at him in mockery by his persecutors (20:3: 'Jeremiah said, YHWH has not named you Pashhur but Magor Misaviv'). The phrase becomes both a prophecy and a lived experience for Jeremiah himself — he becomes the one who lives in terror on every side, embodying the oracle he delivers.
26: bat-ami chigri-saq vehitpallaghshi ba-efer (bat-ʿammî ḥigĕrî-śaq wĕhitpallĕgĕšî bāʾēper): 'O my dear people, put on sackcloth and roll in the ashes; mourn with bitter weeping as for an only child.' Bat ami (daughter of my people) is Jeremiah's most personal address — not the formal 'house of Israel' but his own people, the child of his community. The grief called for is ebel yachid (mourning for an only child) — the most acute form of grief in the ancient world, the grief without replacement or succession. Amos 8:10 uses the same image for eschatological grief: 'I will make it like the mourning for an only son.' The suddenness ('the destroyer is about to come upon us suddenly') prevents the preparation that would allow grief to be managed.
27: bachen nataticha be'ami mivtzar utiyde'a uvachanta et-darkam (bāḥôn nĕtattîkā bĕʿammî miḇṣār wĕtiṯĕdaʿ ûbāḥantā ʾet-darkām): 'I have made you an assayer and a tester among my people, so that you may know and test their conduct.' Bachar/bachen (assayer/tester) is the metallurgical term for testing the purity of metals — Jeremiah is cast in the role of the assay-master who evaluates the quality of the ore. The calling is parallel to Jeremiah's initial call (1:5, 10: 'I appointed you ... to pluck up and tear down ... and to plant and build') but here the evaluative function is foregrounded. The results of the assay follow in vv. 28-30.
28: kullam sarei sorarim holkhei rakhil nechoshet uvarzel kullam mashchitim hem (kullām śārê-sōrĕrîm hōlĕkê-rākîl nĕḥōšet ûbarzel kullām mašḥîtîm hēm): 'They are all rebellious troublemakers, going about spreading slander; they are bronze and iron — utterly corrupt.' The metallurgical assay result: not silver or gold but nechoshet uvarzel (bronze and iron) — base metals that cannot be refined into the precious. The term mashchitim (corrupt/corrupted, the same root as the corruption of Noah's generation in Gen 6:12) is the adjective for something utterly spoiled. The character description — rebellious (sorerim) and slanderous (holkhei rakhil) — adds the social damage they do.
29: naphach mapuach bakesh va'esh tamam areph kesef lashave tzareph (nāpaḥ mappûaḥ bāqeš bāʾēš tāmam ʾārep kesep lešāwĕʾ ṣārēp): 'The bellows blow fiercely; the lead is consumed in the flames; the smelter smelts in vain — the wicked are not drawn off.' The full smelting process: bellows (mappuach) force air to increase heat; lead is added as flux to absorb impurities; the smelter (tzaraph) applies heat to draw off the dross. When the process fails — 'the wicked are not drawn off' (lo nitaqu resha'im) — the ore is ruined. The image of the failed refinery process was taken up in Isa 1:25 ('I will thoroughly purge away your dross') and Ezek 22:18-22 where Israel is described as the dross left after the silver has been removed.
30: kesef nivzah qora'u lahem ki ma'as YHWH bam (kesep nizʾāb qārĕʾû lāhem kî māʾas Yhwh bām): 'They will be called rejected silver, for the LORD has rejected them.' Kesef nivzah (rejected/worthless silver) is the assay verdict: the ore that went through the refinery and came out as waste, not as pure metal. The final word ma'as YHWH bam (the LORD has rejected them) echoes YHWH's rejection of Saul (1 Sam 15:23, 26) and of the Eli priesthood (1 Sam 2:30). Being 'called' rejected silver (qara'u lahem) means the designation is public and permanent — a verdict announced rather than merely felt. The chapter ends on rejection, not judgment — the worse fate is not to be punished but to be assessed as worthless and discarded.
